records.ety: [OE. damnen dampnen (with excrescent p), OF. damner, dampner, F. damner, fr. L. damnare, damnatum, to condemn, fr. damnum damage, a fine, penalty. Cf. Condemn, Damage.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Damned (dmd or dm"nd); p. pr. & vb. n. Damning (dm"ng or dm"nng).]

defs: To condemn; to declare guilty; to doom; to adjudge to punishment; to sentence; to censure.
quotes: He shall not live; look, with a spot I damn him.

defs: To doom to punishment in the future world; to consign to perdition; to curse.
To condemn as bad or displeasing, by open expression, as by denunciation, hissing, hooting, etc.
quotes: You are not so arrant a critic as to damn them [the works of modern poets] . . . without hearing.
Damn with faint praise, assent with civil leer, And without sneering teach the rest to sneer.
notes: ☞ Damn is sometimes used interjectionally, imperatively, and intensively.
